class_schedule.topic: The Web Platform: HTTP, HTML, and the Browser
learning_objectives: Write semantic HTML5 with proper document structure
Use browser developer tools to inspect network requests and DOM
readings: MDN Web Docs: Introduction to HTML

lecture_notes.introduction: The web is the most widely deployed application platform in history. This course teaches you to build for it properly — from semantic HTML to modern JavaScript frameworks to server-side APIs.
key_concepts: HTTP methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH
HTML5 semantic elements: header, nav, main, article, section, footer
The DOM: the browser's tree representation of HTML
Browser developer tools: Network tab, Elements tab, Console
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a semantic HTML page, inspect it with DevTools, and trace a full HTTP request-response cycle using the Network tab
setup_requirements: VS Code with Live Server extension

review.value: Semantic elements (nav, article, aside) communicate meaning to browsers, screen readers, and search engines; using div for everything is technically valid but loses accessibility and SEO benefits
HTTP is stateless: each request is independent with no memory of previous requests. Cookies, sessions, and tokens are mechanisms layered on top to maintain state across requests

learning_objectives: Use Flexbox and CSS Grid for page layout
Implement responsive designs with media queries
Apply CSS custom properties and modern styling techniques
readings: MDN Web Docs: CSS Grid Layout

lecture_notes.introduction: CSS determines how your HTML looks on screen. Flexbox and Grid have replaced float-based hacks, making complex layouts achievable with clean, readable code. Responsive design ensures your layouts work on any screen size.
key_concepts: Flexbox: one-dimensional layout (row or column)
CSS Grid: two-dimensional layout (rows and columns)
Media queries: adapting layout to screen width
Mobile-first design: starting with the smallest screen
CSS custom properties (variables) for consistent theming
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a responsive dashboard layout using CSS Grid for the overall structure and Flexbox for component internals, testing at mobile, tablet, and desktop widths

review.value: Use Flexbox for one-dimensional layouts (a row of cards, a navigation bar); use Grid for two-dimensional layouts (a page layout with sidebar, content, and footer)
Write CSS for the smallest screen first (no media queries), then add min-width media queries for larger screens; this produces simpler CSS and better performance on mobile devices

learning_objectives: Write modern JavaScript using ES6+ features
Manipulate the DOM to create dynamic web pages

lecture_notes.introduction: JavaScript brings web pages to life. It runs in every browser and powers everything from simple form validation to complex single-page applications. Mastering DOM manipulation is the foundation for understanding frameworks.
key_concepts: ES6+ features: let/const, arrow functions, template literals, destructuring
DOM selection: querySelector, querySelectorAll
DOM manipulation: createElement, appendChild, textContent, classList
Event handling: addEventListener, event delegation, event bubbling
Asynchronous JavaScript: Promises, async/await, fetch API
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a dynamic todo list with add, complete, and delete functionality using pure JavaScript DOM manipulation, then refactor to use event delegation

review.value: Instead of attaching event listeners to every child element, attach one listener to the parent and use event.target to identify which child was clicked; this handles dynamically added elements and uses less memory
Syntactic sugar over Promises that makes asynchronous code read like synchronous code; await pauses execution until the Promise resolves, and try/catch handles rejections

class_schedule.topic: React Fundamentals: Components, Props, and State
learning_objectives: Build React applications using functional components and hooks
Pass data between components using props
Manage component state with useState and side effects with useEffect

lecture_notes.introduction: React changed how we build web UIs by introducing a component model where the UI is a pure function of state. Instead of manually updating the DOM, you declare what the UI should look like given the current state, and React handles the updates.
key_concepts: Components: reusable, composable UI building blocks
JSX: JavaScript syntax extension for declaring UI structure
Props: passing data from parent to child components
useState: managing component-local state
useEffect: side effects like data fetching and subscriptions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a weather dashboard component that fetches data from an API on mount, displays current conditions, and allows searching by city name

review.value: In React, you describe what the UI should look like for each state, not how to transition between states; React computes the minimal DOM updates needed, eliminating manual DOM manipulation bugs
Hooks must be called at the top level of a component (not inside loops or conditionals) and only inside React function components or custom hooks; violating these rules causes state corruption

learning_objectives: Build a REST API using Node.js and Express
Implement CRUD operations with proper HTTP methods and status codes
Handle middleware, error handling, and input validation

lecture_notes.introduction: Most web applications need a backend API to manage data, authenticate users, and enforce business rules. Express on Node.js lets you build APIs using JavaScript, keeping the same language across the full stack.
key_concepts: Express routing: app.get(), app.post(), app.put(), app.delete()
Middleware: request processing pipeline (logging, auth, parsing)
Request validation: checking and sanitizing user input
Error handling middleware: centralized error responses
REST principles: resource-based URLs, proper status codes, stateless
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a complete REST API for a bookstore: CRUD for books with input validation, error handling, and request logging middleware
setup_requirements: Postman or Thunder Client for API testing

review.value: Express processes each request through a chain of middleware functions; each can modify the request/response, call next() to continue, or send a response to end the chain
URLs should represent resources (nouns, not verbs): GET /books (list), POST /books (create), GET /books/:id (read), PUT /books/:id (update), DELETE /books/:id (delete)

learning_objectives: Connect a React frontend to an Express backend
Deploy a full-stack application to a cloud platform

lecture_notes.introduction: Connecting frontend and backend into a deployed, authenticated application is where everything comes together. This session covers the last mile: authentication, CORS, environment variables, and deployment.
key_concepts: JWT authentication: token structure, signing, verification
Login flow: credentials -> server -> JWT -> client stores token -> sends with requests
CORS: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing and why it exists
Environment variables: keeping secrets out of code
Deployment: frontend (Vercel/Netlify), backend (Render/Railway)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Add JWT authentication to the bookstore API, build a React login form that stores the token, and deploy both frontend and backend to cloud platforms

review.value: A JWT has three parts: header (algorithm), payload (claims like user ID and expiration), and signature (proves the token was not tampered with); the server verifies the signature without a database lookup
Browsers block requests from one origin to another by default; the server must explicitly allow cross-origin requests by setting Access-Control-Allow-Origin headers for the frontend's domain

questions.question: Write a CSS Grid layout that creates a 3-column, 2-row grid where the first item spans all 3 columns (a header), and the remaining 3 items fill the second row equally.

rubric.key: Correct grid-column span for the header
Proper use of gap and display: grid

questions.question: Explain the difference between event bubbling and event capturing, and describe when you would use event delegation.
questions.answer: Event bubbling: the event starts at the target element and propagates upward through ancestors. Event capturing: the event starts at the document root and propagates downward to the target. By default, listeners fire during bubbling. Event delegation uses bubbling to handle events on dynamically created child elements by placing a single listener on a parent element and checking event.target to determine which child triggered the event. This is more efficient than attaching listeners to each child.

rubric.key: Mentions dynamic elements or efficiency benefit

questions.question: Write a React component that fetches a list of users from /api/users on mount, displays them in a list, and shows a loading state while fetching and an error message if the fetch fails.

rubric.key: Correct useEffect with empty dependency array
Error handling with user-visible error message
Correct rendering with key prop on list items

questions.question: Explain what Express middleware is and describe the execution order when a request arrives at a route with two middleware functions and a route handler.
questions.answer: Express middleware functions receive (req, res, next) and can modify the request/response objects, end the request-response cycle, or call next() to pass control to the next middleware. When a request arrives, Express runs middleware in the order they are registered: first global middleware (app.use()), then route-specific middleware, then the route handler. If middleware1 calls next(), middleware2 runs; if middleware2 calls next(), the route handler runs. If any middleware sends a response without calling next(), the chain stops.

rubric.key: Explains that next() passes control and omitting it stops the chain

major_assignments.title: Full-Stack Web Application
major_assignments.description: Build and deploy a complete full-stack web application with a React frontend, Express backend, database persistence, user authentication, and responsive design. The application should solve a real problem and be deployed to production.
objectives: Build a responsive React frontend with multiple routes and components
Implement a RESTful Express API with CRUD operations and authentication
Persist data using a database (PostgreSQL or MongoDB)
Deploy the complete application to cloud platforms
deliverables: Deployed web application accessible via public URL
GitHub repository with frontend and backend code
Design document (2 pages) explaining architecture and technology choices

criteria: React components are well-structured and reusable
UI is responsive across mobile, tablet, and desktop
Loading states and error messages are displayed to the user
Client-side routing works correctly
REST API follows proper HTTP method and status code conventions
Input validation prevents invalid data from reaching the database
Authentication protects routes that require login
Error handling returns clear, consistent error responses
Application is deployed and accessible via public URL
Environment variables used for secrets (no hardcoded keys)
Database is hosted and persists data between deployments
Application handles errors gracefully in production
API documentation covers all endpoints
Design document explains architecture decisions
README includes setup instructions for local development
Screen recording shows all major features
Complete full-stack application with React frontend and Express backend
Working authentication, CRUD operations, and responsive design
Deployed to production with proper environment configuration
Thorough documentation including API docs and architecture rationale

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of web technologies and concepts

midterm_examination.description: Written and coding exam covering frontend technologies

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all lectures and mandatory for lab sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences may result in a reduced participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of web development concepts is encouraged. All code must be written individually unless pair programming is explicitly assigned.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted code must be your own work. Using templates, starter kits, or AI-generated code without attribution is plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need Node.js 20+, VS Code, Chrome/Firefox with DevTools, and accounts on GitHub, Vercel, and a database hosting provider.
